resources.title: Abdullah's Tutorials
resources.description: Video series on FRC Java programming: command-based structure, subsystem examples, control tuning, and competition code organization.

resources.title: Advanced Debugging
resources.description: Advanced debugging in Android Studio is essential for FTC development.  When your robot doesn't behave as expected, you need powerful tools to identify and fix issues quickly.

resources.title: Advanced Movement Control
resources.description: FTC robots often need to move with precision, repeatability, and speed.  Simple power commands are not enough for tasks like driving straight, turning to a specific angle, or moving an arm to an exact position.

resources.title: Advanced Programming Notes
resources.description: Community FRC programming notes covering project structure, command-based patterns, control loops, and common subsystem implementations with worked examples.

resources.title: AdvantageKit Swerve Template
resources.description: AdvantageKit Talon FX swerve template: pre-wired odometry, module optimization, SysId hooks, and log replay for CTRE swerve drivetrains.

resources.title: Android Studio Official Site
resources.description: Download Android Studio IDE, SDK Manager, and official guides for installing the Android SDK used to build and deploy FTC Robot Controller apps.

resources.title: Auto Align Tutorial
resources.description: Step-by-step reef auto-align using Limelight target offsets, rotation-first alignment, and PID drive commands — includes code structure and tuning notes.

resources.title: Autonomous Programming (Video)
resources.description: Video intro to FRC autonomous: timed sequences vs sensor-based moves, chooser setup, and when to add path following to a baseline auto.

resources.title: Basic Autonomous Programming
resources.description: In FTC, autonomous mode is a period at the start of each match where the robot operates entirely based on pre-programmed instructions, without any driver input.  This is your opportunity to score points by having your robot perform tasks automatically.

resources.title: Basic Debugging Techniques
resources.description: Debugging is a critical skill for FTC programmers.  In this lesson, you'll learn how to use OnBot Java's tools and best practices to find and fix bugs in your robot code.

resources.title: BoVLB Tips: Commands
resources.description: Oblarg (BoVLB) command tips: default commands, requirement conflicts, parallel and sequential command groups, and common command-based pitfalls with concise examples.

resources.title: CD Tuning Guide
resources.description: Chief Delphi PID tuning thread: practical kP/kI/kD workflow on real mechanisms, oscillation diagnosis, and when feedforward matters more than integral gain.

resources.title: Code Organization
resources.description: Good code organization makes your programs easier to debug, maintain, and expand.  In FTC, it's especially important to avoid large, monolithic files.

resources.title: Code Review
resources.description: Code review is a systematic process of examining code to find bugs, improve quality, and ensure consistency.  For FTC teams, code reviews help catch issues early and improve team collaboration.

resources.title: Code Version Control
resources.description: Version control is a system that tracks changes to your code over time.  It lets you save your work, collaborate with teammates, and revert to previous versions if something goes wrong.

resources.title: Control Hub & Expansion Hub
resources.description: Mantik lesson on the REV Control Hub and Expansion Hub: USB/device naming, motor and servo ports, I2C sensors, configuration on Driver Hub, and wiring checks before running OpModes.

resources.title: CTRE Control Requests
resources.description: Phoenix 6 control requests: DutyCycle, Velocity, Position, Motion Magic, and TorqueCurrent — how to replace legacy set() calls with explicit request objects.

resources.title: CTRE Documentation
resources.description: CTRE Phoenix 6 documentation for Talon FX, CANcoder, and Pigeon — configuration, control requests, Motion Magic, telemetry, and migration from Phoenix 5.

resources.title: Custom Path Following Algorithms
resources.description: While libraries like Road Runner and PedroPathing are powerful, building your own path following algorithm helps you understand the math and control theory behind autonomous navigation.  It also lets you customize behavior for your robot's unique needs.

resources.title: DC Motors and Motor Control
resources.description: DC motors drive wheels, arms, and other moving parts.  Access motors through the `hardwareMap`—an FTC SDK object that provides references to configured devices.

resources.title: Dead Wheel Encoders
resources.description: Dead wheels are unpowered, free-spinning wheels equipped with encoders.  Unlike drive wheels, they are not affected by slippage from driving the robot, making them ideal for accurate position tracking in FTC robots.

resources.title: Debugging Strategies
resources.description: Effective debugging is not just about finding bugs—it's about developing a systematic approach to problem-solving.  In FTC development, you need strategies that work both in the lab and under competition pressure.

resources.title: Deep Blue Robotics
resources.description: Deep Blue (Team 384) training docs: swerve odometry, auto routines, command-based conventions, and advanced control topics used in their open-source projects.

resources.title: Drivebase Types
resources.description: The drivebase is the foundation of your robot's movement.  Common FTC drivebases are tank, mecanum, and holonomic.

resources.title: Encoder-Based Movement
resources.description: Motor encoders are sensors built into motors that measure rotation.  In FTC, encoders allow you to move your robot a precise distance by counting the number of rotations or partial rotations of the wheels.

resources.title: EveryBot Commands Intro
resources.description: EveryBot code manual: starter command-based project layout, intake and elevator examples, and patterns used in low-resource EveryBot build guides.

resources.title: Exponential Profiles (YAMS)
resources.description: YAMS exponential motion profiles: configure max velocity/acceleration and generate smooth setpoints for mechanisms that need curved velocity ramps.

resources.title: External Libraries in FTC
resources.description: FTC teams often use external libraries to simplify complex programming tasks and improve code organization.  These libraries provide pre-built solutions for common robotics problems, allowing teams to focus on strategy and robot-specific logic rather than reinventing basic functionality.

resources.title: FRC Git Tutorial
resources.description: Slide deck on FRC Git workflow: branches, remotes, merge conflicts in robot projects, and coordinating code changes across subteams.

resources.title: FTC Android Studio Tutorial
resources.description: FIRST FTC Android Studio tutorial: install SDK, clone FtcRobotController, open TeamCode, deploy OpModes, and connect to Control Hub over ADB/Wi‑Fi.

resources.title: FTC OpMode Documentation
resources.description: FTC OpMode reference: @TeleOp vs @Autonomous, linear vs iterative lifecycle, hardware map access, and gamepad/telemetry patterns in the SDK.

resources.title: FTC SDK Basics
resources.description: The FTC (FIRST Tech Challenge) Software Development Kit (SDK) is the foundation for programming FTC robots in Java.  It provides all the libraries, APIs, and tools needed to control robot hardware, manage OpModes, and interact with sensors and gamepads.

resources.title: FTC SDK GitHub Repository
resources.description: Official FTC SDK repository: FtcRobotController module (do not edit) and TeamCode template for season-specific OpModes, Gradle build, and OnBot Java compatibility.

resources.title: FTCDashboard Comprehensive Guide
resources.description: FTCDashboard is a powerful web-based dashboard that provides real-time robot monitoring and control capabilities for FTC teams.  Unlike basic telemetry that only shows text output, FTCDashboard offers a rich graphical interface with live data plotting, field visualization, configuration management, remote control features, and camera streaming.

resources.title: Gamepad Controls
resources.description: FTC robots are controlled using gamepads.  The `gamepad1` and `gamepad2` objects provide access to all the buttons, sticks, triggers, and dpad controls.

resources.title: Gobilda Pinpoint
resources.description: goBILDA Pinpoint is a commercial odometry solution designed for FTC robots.  It provides a ready-to-use hardware kit with three encoders and wheels, making it easier to implement accurate odometry without custom fabrication.

resources.title: Introduction to Odometry
resources.description: Odometry is the process of tracking a robot’s position and orientation on the field using sensors, typically wheel encoders.  In FTC, odometry is essential for enabling your robot to navigate autonomously and perform tasks with precision.

resources.title: Limelight Documentation
resources.description: Limelight 2+/3 docs: static IP setup, pipeline tuning, AprilTag and retroreflective tracking, robot pose estimation, and NetworkTables integration.

resources.title: Limelight Lib
resources.description: LimelightHelpers / LimelightLib: Java API for pose estimates, target alignment, and pipeline data over NetworkTables without writing raw HTTP or JSON parsers.

resources.title: Linear vs Iterative OpModes
resources.description: FTC supports two main OpMode types: LinearOpMode and Iterative OpMode.  Understanding the difference helps you choose the right structure for your code.

resources.title: Motion Magic®
resources.description: CTRE Motion Magic: trapezoidal motion profiling on Talon FX with cruise velocity, acceleration, and jerk limits for smooth mechanism moves without a separate profile generator.

resources.title: Motion Profiling
resources.description: Motion profiling is a technique for planning smooth, efficient movements for your robot.  Instead of instantly commanding full speed or power, you generate a profile that gradually accelerates and decelerates, making your robot's motion faster, more accurate, and less likely to slip or tip.

resources.title: OpMode Structure
resources.description: An OpMode (Operation Mode) is a Java class that controls your robot during a match.  OpModes define the robot's behavior for autonomous or teleop periods and are selected and started from the Driver Station app.

resources.title: PathPlanner Docs
resources.description: PathPlanner hub: GUI path authoring, PathPlannerLib API for holonomic and differential autos, event markers, and integration with WPILib pose estimation.

resources.title: PathPlanner Getting Started
resources.description: PathPlanner GUI tutorial: draw paths and constraints, set ideal starting pose, export to deploy folder, and wire PathPlannerAuto in RobotContainer.

resources.title: PedroPathing vs. Road Runner
resources.description: FTC teams use path planning libraries to generate and follow complex autonomous paths.  Two popular options are PedroPathing and Road Runner.

resources.title: Performance Optimization
resources.description: Performance optimization is crucial for FTC robots.  Your code must run efficiently to maintain consistent control loop timing and prevent crashes.

resources.title: PID Control
resources.description: PID stands for Proportional-Integral-Derivative.  It's a feedback control algorithm that helps your robot reach and maintain a target value (like position or speed) by adjusting motor power based on the error between the target and the actual value.

resources.title: PID Simulation
resources.description: Hands-on PID simulation exercises using WPILib physics sim — tune elevator and arm gains with recorded plots before moving to hardware.

resources.title: Poses, Paths, and Theory
resources.description: A pose describes a robot's position and orientation on the field.  In FTC, a pose is usually represented as (x, y, θ), where x and y are coordinates (in inches or meters) and θ (theta) is the heading (angle) in radians or degrees.

resources.title: Problem Solving Guide | FRC 167
resources.description: Team 167 Iowa City robotics programming guide: debugging workflow, Git usage, command-based structure, and subsystem design patterns for competition code.

resources.title: Pull Requests Overview
resources.description: Pull request walkthrough: fork/branch workflow, opening a PR on GitHub, review comments, and merging safely into a shared robot repository.

resources.title: REV Robotics API
resources.description: REV Java API reference for Spark Max, Spark Flex, and REV sensors — motor configuration, closed-loop control, encoder interfaces, and CAN bus usage.

resources.title: Robot PID Control
resources.description: Many FTC mechanisms require controlling more than one axis at a time, such as X/Y drive or multi-joint arms.  Each axis can have its own PID controller, and sometimes you need to coordinate them for smooth, accurate movement.

resources.title: Sensors
resources.description: Sensors let the robot perceive its environment.  Configure each sensor in the robot's hardware configuration, then map it in code.

resources.title: Servos
resources.description: Servos provide precise, limited-range movement for arms, claws, and similar mechanisms.  Standard servos move to a position and hold it.

resources.title: Simple Limelight Tutorial
resources.description: Video walkthrough: Limelight pipeline setup, reading tx/ty/ ta in code, and driving rotation-to-target for vision-assisted teleop or auto.

resources.title: Software Development Best Practices
resources.description: Good software development practices are essential for FTC teams.  Well-organized, maintainable code is easier to debug, extend, and collaborate on.

resources.title: Spectrum Training
resources.description: Spectrum 3847 team site: technical blog posts, CAD/software release notes, and links to their training materials and open repositories.

resources.title: State Machines
resources.description: State machines are a powerful way to organize your robot's logic, especially for complex tasks.  They help you avoid tangled code and make debugging much easier.

resources.title: State Machines Intro
resources.description: State machine concepts for FRC: states, transitions, entry/exit actions, and replacing nested if-chains in teleop and auto with explicit state charts.

resources.title: Telemetry & Feedback
resources.description: This page covers advanced telemetry techniques for FTC robots.  If you are new to telemetry, start with the Telemetry Logging lesson for an introduction to basic telemetry concepts and usage.

resources.title: Telemetry Logging
resources.description: Mantik lesson on FTC telemetry: addLine, addData, update(), formatting driver feedback on the Driver Station, and debugging sensor values during teleop and auto.

resources.title: TeleOp Programming
resources.description: TeleOp programming is the process of writing code that allows drivers to control the robot in real time using gamepads.  In this lesson, you'll learn how to read gamepad input, map controls to robot actions, and use telemetry for feedback.

resources.title: TeleOp Programming Exercises
resources.description: Hands-on practice is essential for mastering TeleOp programming.  These exercises are designed to reinforce and apply the concepts you've learned in previous lessons.

resources.title: The Compass Alliance
resources.description: Compass Alliance curriculum: staged FRC learning paths from mechanical basics through programming, scouting, and team management resources.

resources.title: Three-Wheel Odometry
resources.description: A three-wheel odometry setup allows your robot to track its position (X, Y) and heading (angle) on the field with high accuracy.  By using two parallel wheels and one perpendicular wheel, you can resolve both translational and rotational movement.

resources.title: Time-Based Movement
resources.description: Time-based movement is the simplest way to make your robot move autonomously: you set the motor power, wait for a certain amount of time, then stop the motors.  This method does not use sensors or encoders, so it is easy to implement but not very precise.

resources.title: Tracking AprilTags
resources.description: Limelight AprilTag pipelines: family selection, multi-tag pose solve, field layout JSON, and tuning exposure for reliable FRC field localization.

resources.title: Troubleshooting Common Issues
resources.description: Even experienced programmers run into common issues when working with FTC robots.  This lesson will help you quickly identify and fix the most frequent problems in OnBot Java.

resources.title: Unit Testing
resources.description: Unit testing is a critical practice for ensuring your FTC robot code works correctly and reliably.  By writing tests for individual components and methods, you can catch bugs early, verify functionality, and make your code more maintainable.

resources.title: WPILib Documentation
resources.description: Official WPILib docs: robot project setup, command-based framework, control theory, kinematics, vision, simulation, and hardware APIs for FRC Java/C++ teams.

resources.title: WPILib: Git Intro
resources.description: WPILib Git intro: clone WPILib projects, commit workflow, .gitignore for build artifacts, and collaborating without breaking Gradle vendor deps.

resources.title: WPILib: What Is Command-Based?
resources.description: WPILib explains command-based architecture — Subsystems, Commands, triggers, and RobotContainer — and why it separates robot logic from scheduling.

resources.title: YAMS Documentation
resources.description: YAMS (Yet Another Motor Simulator): WPILib mechanism sim for elevators, arms, and intakes — plant tuning, visualization, and validating gains before deploy.
